FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, n=9,208 children 9-10 years old, baseline 2016-2018, 2-year follow-up 2018-2020

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying smartphone variables: TV shows/movies, videos (YouTube), video games, texting, video chat, social media. All are qualifying per B1-B7. Total screen time with per-modality breakdown reported; applies B1 (separate β/OR for multiple modalities)

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression (Model 3): aOR 1.05 (95% CI 1.01-1.09) for total screen time; aOR 1.15 (95% CI 1.03-1.28) for video games; aOR 1.11 (95% CI 1.01-1.23) for watching videos at 2-year follow-up

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 3 reports adjusted odds ratios (aOR) with 95% confidence intervals for all screen modalities and total screen time in Models 1-3

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD prospective analysis: baseline screen time exposure (9-10 years) predicting OCD diagnosis at 2-year follow-up (11-12 years)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Adolescent Health (2023); no evidence of duplicate preprint or earlier publication of this analysis

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is independent variable (IV) predicting OCD as dependent variable (DV), not demographic predictor of SM. EC7 requires SM as DV with only demographic predictors. Does not apply per condition (a) violation.
notes: All three papers in batch are original Nagata et al. ABCD studies with different outcomes (OCD, sleep, conduct/oppositional defiant disorder). Paper 133 analyzes screen time as IV predicting psychiatric outcome (OCD), so FT-EC7 does not apply.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, n=10,280 early adolescents aged 10-14, Year 2 data (2018-2020)

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying smartphone/SM variables: TV/movies, video games, music, phone/text, social media, chat rooms, internet browsing, computer/laptop use for studying, reading, phone ringer status, device in bedroom. All constitute qualifying SM or smartphone use per B1-B7. Separate coefficients reported for each modality (applies B1).

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression: ARR 1.27 (95% CI 1.12-1.44) for device in bedroom and trouble falling/staying asleep; ARR 1.23 (95% CI 1.03-1.46) for leaving ringer on; ARRs 1.08-1.27 (95% CI) for various bedtime screen activities

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 3 reports adjusted risk ratios (ARR) with 95% confidence intervals for all bedtime screen activities and device usage; Table 1 shows sample characteristics and prevalence rates

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD cross-sectional analysis (Year 2): bedtime screen use behaviors associated with self-reported and caregiver-reported sleep outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Sleep Health (2023); no evidence of duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is independent variable (IV) predicting sleep outcomes as dependent variable (DV), not SM as DV. Cross-sectional design. FT-EC7 requires SM as DV with longitudinal demographics-only predictors. Does not apply per condition (a) violation.
notes: Cross-sectional bedtime screen use analysis. Screen usage is IV predicting sleep disturbance outcomes. FT-EC7 excludes because SM/screen time is not the dependent variable.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, N=11,875 (analysis sample after exclusions), children 9-11 years old at baseline (2016-2018), 1-year follow-up (2017-2019)

FT_IC2.evidence: Multiple qualifying smartphone/SM variables: total screen time, television/movies, videos, video games, social media, video chat, texting. Each reported as separate modality with own coefficient. Applies B1 (separate β/PR for SM, texting, video chat, video games reported with own coefficients; conduct disorder strongest for social media at PR 1.62, 95% CI 1.39-1.87).

FT_IC3.evidence: Modified Poisson regression: PR 1.07 (95% CI 1.03-1.11) for total screen time and conduct disorder; PR 1.62 (95% CI 1.39-1.87) for social media and conduct disorder; PR 1.21, 1.19, 1.17, 1.14 for video chat, texting, TV/movies, video games respectively predicting oppositional defiant disorder

FT_IC4.evidence: Results section reports prevalence ratios (PR) with 95% confidence intervals for all screen modalities predicting conduct and oppositional defiant disorder

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD prospective cohort analysis: baseline screen time (age 9-11) predicting parent-reported conduct/oppositional defiant disorder at 1-year follow-up based on KSADS-5

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry (2023); no evidence of duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is independent variable (IV) predicting disruptive behavior disorders (conduct disorder, oppositional defiant disorder) as dependent variables (DV), not SM as DV. FT-EC7 requires SM as DV with only demographic predictors. Does not apply per condition (a) violation.
notes: Prospective analysis with 1-year follow-up. Screen time/SM reported as separate modalities with individual coefficients, meeting B1. Strongest association is social media with conduct disorder (PR 1.62). FT-EC7 does not apply because screen time/SM is the exposure (IV), not outcome (DV).
